summary:
The bill’s primary purpose is to ensure that Department of Health and Human Services grant program materials addressing opioid misuse and use disorders do not treat naloxone as the only opioid overdose reversal drug.

The bill directs the Secretary of Health and Human Services, when issuing regulations or guidance for any HHS grant program addressing opioid misuse and use disorders, to ensure that any reference to an opioid overdose reversal drug, including a reference to naloxone, is interpreted to include any opioid overdose reversal drug approved under section 505 of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act for emergency treatment of a known or suspected opioid overdose.

For existing HHS regulations or guidance, the bill requires the Secretary to update covered references within one year after enactment. This update must make those references inclusive of any opioid overdose reversal drug that has been approved or otherwise authorized for use by the Food and Drug Administration. The requirement applies only to references issued before enactment that appear in two specified grant programs: the State and Tribal response to opioid use disorders program under the 21st Century Cures Act, commonly known as State Opioid Response Grants and Tribal Opioid Response Grants, and the grant program for priority substance use disorder prevention needs of regional and national significance under the Public Health Service Act.

The bill is titled the “Halting the Epidemic of Addiction and Loss Act of 2025.”
